claims.claim: In a controlled synthetic setting—a sparse linear teacher with 6 salient features among 64 dimensions, plus correlated distractors—we compare label-only, logit KD, saliency KD, and combined logit-plus-saliency distillation across five training-set sizes (16–256 samples) with 20 random seeds each.

claims.claim: Saliency KD consistently recovered the teacher's true feature support (top-6 precision = 1.0 at all training sizes) and improved accuracy over label-only baselines by +23.5 to +9.1 percentage points, with the largest gains under severe label scarcity.

claims.claim: If the teacher can provide not just output predictions but also *where it looks*—i.e., which input dimensions matter—could that attribution information guide the student toward the correct feature support more efficiently than labels or logits alone?

claims.claim: We define a binary classification task with $d = 64$ input dimensions.

evidence_refs.quote: Knowledge distillation conventionally transfers knowledge via soft logits or intermediate feature maps. We investigate whether teacher-provided saliency (attribution) maps can serve as useful auxiliary distillation targets when a student model has insufficient labels to reliably identify the correct input features.
